name: First Steps CLI App
description: Build a small command-line Python app that prints your name and a welcome message.
instructions: Create a Python file called main.py. Store your name in a variable, print your name, and print then the welcome line. Run it from the terminal.
goals: Create and run a Python file
steps: Create a file named main.py
Store your name in a variable
Print the variable
Print a second line
Run the file in your terminal
deliverables: A Python file that runs without errors
starterTips: Keep it simple, the goal is confidence and setup
Use print(name) rather than typing the name twice
Edit the welcome text and rerun the script to test changes

stages.label: Print your name and welcome message

name: Mini Mad Libs Generator
description: Collect words from the user to generate a funny sentence.
instructions: Ask the user for a values such as a name, verbs, adjectives, and a place. Then print a silly story using those values.
goals: Combining them in a single output
steps: Choose 4 or 5 words to collect from the user input
Store each response in a variable
Build a final sentence using all responses
Finally print the result for a funny story
deliverables: A script that asks for several user inputs
A generated sentence or short story
starterTips: Keep the story short and funny
You can join text with commas in print() or use +

stages.label: Read the brief and choose your word prompts

name: Build a Calculator
description: Create a simple Python calculator that can do basic arithmetic operations.
instructions: Build a calculator program in Python. Ask the user for two numbers and an operation, then print the result. Make sure to handle invalid operations.
steps: Ask the user for the first number
Ask the user for the second number
Ask for an operator like +, -, * or /
Use if / elif / else to decide what to do
Print the result in a readable format
deliverables: Support for at least four maths operations
starterTips: Start without functions, then refactor into functions later
Remember input() returns text, so convert numbers using int() or float()

stages.label: Read the brief and plan the supported operations
Run at least 3 successful test cases

name: Number Guessing Game
description: Build a guessing game where the player tries to guess the hidden number.
instructions: Choose a target number either randomly or hardcoded. Collect user guesses from input. Keep the game going until the player gets the number right.
goals: Use a loop for repeated guesses
Compare each guess against a target
Give feedback like too high or too low
steps: Choose a target number
Ask the user for an initial guess
Use a while loop until the guess is correct
Print hints after wrong guesses
Print a nice celebrate message when the number is found
deliverables: A playable guessing game in the terminal
A success message at the end
starterTips: You can get started with a hardcoded or fixed target, then randomize it later
Think carefully about when and how the loop should end

stages.label: End the game correctly on success

name: Study Score Tracker
description: Store study scores in a list. Use them to calculate useful aggregates such the average score or best score and lowest scores.
instructions: Create a list of study scores and write logic that prints stats about the list.
goals: Work with a list of numbers
steps: Create a list of at least 5 scores
Calculate the total
Compute the average
Find the highest and lowest score
Print a simple report
deliverables: The average, the max, and the min output
starterTips: You can try manually first, then refactor with loops

name: Simple Quiz Engine
description: Develop a mini quiz game. It should have at least three questions and a counter to keep scores.
instructions: Store the questions and answers, ask the player to answer each question. Use the counter to track how many they get right.
steps: Create at least 3 questions
Store answers in a clean structure
Ask each question one at a time
Check user input against correct answers
Print a score summary
deliverables: A quiz with at least 3 questions
starterTips: You can begin with parallel lists, then upgrade the structure to use dictionaries
Think about including lowercase comparison for easier matching
